common.title: DevTools Suite
common.description: Modern developer tools for everyday tasks.

home.heading: Developer Tools
home.subheading: Simple, fast, and secure tools for your development workflow.
home.no_results_desc: We couldn't find any tools matching your search or filters.

adler32.name: Adler32 Checksum

hints.bits: Higher bit counts offer more security but slower processing. 2048 is standard; 4096 is high-security.
hints.algorithm: The cryptographic algorithm used to generate the key pair.
hints.cn: The primary name the certificate is issued for (e.g., your domain or server name).
hints.org: The legal name of your organization.
hints.unit: The department or sub-unit within the organization.
hints.pass: The password required to import the certificate and private key into other systems.
hints.p7_content: The message or data you want to sign or wrap in the PKCS#7 container.
hints.salt: A unique string added to the password to prevent pre-computed rainbow table attacks.
hints.hmac_key: The private key used to sign the message.
hints.validity_years: Number of years until the certificate expires.
hints.aes_key: Must be 16, 24, or 32 characters long (128, 192, or 256 bits).
hints.aes_iv: Usually 16 characters (128 bits). Used to ensure the same plaintext encrypts to different ciphertext.
hints.aes_mode: GCM is recommended for modern security as it provides authentication. CBC is standard for legacy systems.
hints.des_key: Must be exactly 8 characters (64 bits). DES is legacy and considered insecure.
hints.3des_key: Must be exactly 24 characters (192 bits). Used for Triple Data Encryption Standard.
hints.chacha20_key: Should be 32 characters (256 bits) for maximum security.
hints.chacha20_iv: A Nonce, usually 12 characters (96 bits) for standard RFC7539 implementation.
hints.pass_gen_similar: Avoid visually similar characters like 'i' and 'l' or '0' and 'o'.

compare.valid_proof: The provided plaintext successfully verifies against the cryptographic hash.
compare.invalid_proof: Verification failed. The plaintext does not match the provided hash.

kb.md5: MD5 produces a 128-bit hash. It's fast but cryptographically broken. Use only for integrity checks, never for security or passwords.
kb.sha1: SHA-1 produces a 160-bit hash. It's now considered weak and susceptible to collision attacks. Primarily used for legacy system compatibility.
kb.sha256: SHA-256 is part of the SHA-2 family. It's widely used in security protocols (SSL/TLS) and blockchain technology. Currently considered highly secure.
kb.sha512: SHA-512 offers a larger 512-bit digest. It provides extremely high security and is often faster on 64-bit architectures than SHA-256.
kb.sha3: SHA-3 is the latest NIST standard. It uses a completely different internal structure (Sponge construction) than SHA-2, providing extra safety against current SHA-2 weaknesses.
kb.hmac_sha1: HMAC-SHA1 uses the SHA-1 hash function. While SHA-1 is weak for collisions, HMAC-SHA1 is still considered mathematically strong for authentication, though modern systems prefer SHA-256.
kb.hmac_sha256: HMAC-SHA256 is the industry standard for message authentication. It's used in JWT (HS256), AWS signing, and many secure APIs.
kb.hmac_sha512: HMAC-SHA512 provides the highest level of security in the HMAC family, often used in high-security financial or military systems.

bcrypt.title: Bcrypt: Adaptive Hashing
bcrypt.desc: Bcrypt is a battle-tested algorithm that uses a configurable 'cost factor' to slow down attackers. It stores the salt and cost inside the resulting hash string.
bcrypt.note: Standard cost is 10-12. Going too high significantly increases computation time.

argon2.title: Argon2: The Modern Winner
argon2.desc: Winner of the Password Hashing Competition. It is designed to resist GPU/ASIC cracking by being 'memory-hard'.
argon2.note: Argon2id is the recommended version for most use cases.

pbkdf2.title: PBKDF2: Standard Derivation
pbkdf2.desc: Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2. It applies a pseudo-random function (like HMAC-SHA256) many times to make brute-force slow.
pbkdf2.note: NIST recommends at least 600,000 iterations for PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256.

kb.hmac_general: HMAC is superior to a simple hash for verification because even if an attacker changes the message, they cannot reproduce the HMAC without your Secret Key.

aes.title: AES: Symmetric Standard
aes.desc: Advanced Encryption Standard is the most widely used symmetric algorithm globally. It uses the same key for both encryption and decryption.
aes.note: GCM mode is preferred because it provides both confidentiality and integrity (AEAD).

des.title: DES: Legacy Encryption
des.desc: Data Encryption Standard was published in 1977. It uses a 56-bit effective key length which is easily broken by modern brute force.
des.note: Use only for legacy file decryption. Never use for new data.

3des.title: 3DES: Triple Security
3des.desc: Triple DES runs the DES algorithm three times with different keys. It was a bridge between DES and AES.
3des.note: Considered legacy but still seen in banking and smart cards.

chacha20.title: ChaCha20: Modern Stream Cipher
chacha20.desc: ChaCha20 is a high-speed stream cipher that is often faster than AES on platforms without hardware acceleration.
chacha20.note: Extensively used in TLS 1.3 and mobile communications.

kb.crc32: CRC-32 is a cyclic redundancy check used to detect accidental changes to raw data. It is widely used in ZIP files and Ethernet frames. Note: It is NOT cryptographically secure.
kb.adler32: Adler-32 is a checksum algorithm which was invented by Mark Adler. It is almost as reliable as CRC-32 but can be computed much more quickly in software.

p1.title: PKCS#1: RSA Cryptography
p1.desc: Specific to RSA keys. Defines the structure of public/private keys and padding schemes.
p1.i1_text: Used in RSA encryption (v1.5 or OAEP) to prevent chosen-ciphertext attacks.
p1.i2_text: Standard format for RSA-specific key pairs (RSAPrivateKey/RSAPublicKey).

p7.title: PKCS#7: Cryptographic Message Syntax
p7.desc: Used for signing and/or encrypting data. Often used in S/MIME or code signing.
p7.i1_text: Used for Block Ciphers (AES/DES). Pads data to match the block size (e.g., 16 bytes).
p7.i2_text: Can wrap certificates, CRLs, and signed data into a single CMS container.

p8.title: PKCS#8: Private-Key Information
p8.desc: A generic syntax for private keys that supports multiple algorithms (RSA, EC, etc.).
p8.i1_text: Unlike PKCS#1, it wraps the key with an OID that identifies the algorithm.
p8.i2_text: Can optionally be encrypted (EncryptedPrivateKeyInfo) with a password.

p10.title: PKCS#10: Certification Request (CSR)
p10.desc: A request sent to a Certificate Authority (CA) to sign a public key.
p10.i1_text: Includes identity details like Common Name (CN), Org (O), and Country (C).
p10.i2_text: The CSR is self-signed by the private key to prove ownership of the public key.

p12.title: PKCS#12: Personal Information (PFX)
p12.desc: A binary container (PFX) used to store certificates and their matching private keys.
p12.i1_text: Includes the end-entity certificate, the private key, and the CA chain.
p12.i2_text: The container is always password-protected to secure the private key during transit.

x509.title: X.509: Digital Certificates
x509.desc: The standard format for public key certificates used in HTTPS/TLS and other protocols.
x509.i1_text: A certificate where the issuer and subject are the same. Used for internal testing.
x509.i2_text: Defines the validity dates between which the certificate is considered valid.
x509.i3_text: Additional metadata like Subject Alternative Name (SAN) for supporting multiple domains.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.card1_desc: All tools run entirely in your browser. Your data never leaves your computer.
privacy.card2_desc: We do not collect, store, or share any personal information or input data.
privacy.s1_desc: DevTools Suite is designed as a stateless utility. We do not require account registration, and we do not have a backend database to store your inputs. Whether you are hashing a password or formatting JSON, that data stays in your browser's memory.
privacy.s2_desc: We may use basic, anonymous analytical tools to understand which tools are most popular. This data is purely numerical and contains no identifiable user information or payload data.
privacy.s3_desc: We do not sell, trade, or otherwise transfer your information to outside parties.
privacy.s4_desc: By performing all operations locally via JavaScript, we eliminate the primary security risk of data interception during transit to a server.

terms.title: Terms of Service
terms.agreement_desc: By accessing DevTools Suite, you agree to be bound by these terms. These tools are provided for educational and professional development purposes.
terms.p1_desc: You are free to use these tools for personal or commercial projects. There are no fees or subscriptions.
terms.p2_desc: The tools are provided without warranties of any kind. You should verify critical outputs for production systems.
terms.p3_desc: You are responsible for the data you input. Since we do not store your data, we cannot recover any lost keys once you close your browser session.
terms.liability_desc: In no event shall DevTools Suite be liable for any damages arising out of the use or inability to use the tools on this website.

cookies.title: Cookie Policy
cookies.subtitle: How we use local storage and cookies.
cookies.c1_desc: We do not use advertising or tracking cookies.
cookies.c2_desc: Used only to remember your theme and language preferences.
cookies.details_intro: DevTools Suite uses minimal data stored on your device to provide a consistent user experience.
cookies.s1_item3: Sidebar State: A small cookie used to remember layout settings.
cookies.s2_title: 2. Why we don't show a banner
cookies.s2_desc: According to global privacy laws, explicit consent is generally required for non-essential tracking. Since our data storage is strictly functional and essential, a intrusive pop-up banner is not legally required.
cookies.s3_desc: You can clear these preferences at any time by clearing your browser's site data.
